summary: Patrick describes a common usability problem with software screenshot pages: customers click thumbnail images and get lost viewing the full-size image, unable to navigate back. He notes his screenshots had 80%+ bounce rates until he addressed this. He recommends Lightbox, a JavaScript widget that overlays the screenshot on the current page with a grayed-out background, keeping the sales context visible. He chose the simpler Lightbox over Lightbox2, which he found to be a usability nightmare for his non-technical audience.
key_points: Screenshot thumbnails on software sites exist more to prove a real product exists than to convey detailed information
Non-technical customers often cannot navigate back from a full-size screenshot image, causing 80%+ bounce rates
Opening screenshots in new windows is a compromise but distracts from the sales pitch and risks losing the customer entirely
Lightbox overlays screenshots while keeping the underlying page visible, maintaining sales context and enabling easy dismissal
Simpler tools often beat feature-rich alternatives when your audience is non-technical
money_quotes: These are generally teeny-tiny thumbnails which exist more to demonstrate the fact that there is a real purchasable product than anything else.
For the non-technical B2C market, 'bad stuff' generally means 'Prospective customer cannot find their way out of the image'.
Yes, I know, I know, they have a back button. They may not KNOW they have a back button, though.
My screenshots, for the longest time, had 80%+ bounce rates until I figured this out.
I'd rather they have a visual reminder of what exactly they were doing so they don't get distracted, switch to their email, and go away.
key_insight: Every UI element on your sales page should keep customers moving toward purchase, and even something as mundane as screenshot presentation can cause massive bounce rates if it breaks the customer's flow.
